query: How can a group of servers all settle on the same value when some of them crash or messages arrive late, without relying on a central boss?
Since machine wall clocks drift apart, how can a distributed system figure out whether one event caused another or whether two updates happened independently?
When a client isn't sure if its payment request went through, how do you let it resend safely so the work only happens once?
What do you do when a producer pushes data faster than the downstream consumer can handle, to stop queues from growing without bound?
What does the FENCING_EPOCH_ID guard value do and why is it attached to writes?
During an overload, aggressive client retries make congestion even worse. What two mechanisms work together to handle this, one for making retries harmless and one for throttling the flow of work?
How can a database recover a consistent state after a sudden power loss or crash by saving change intentions to a journal first?
What technique keeps multiple copies of each row so that reading transactions never have to wait on writing ones and vice versa?
Why is reusing a set of already-open database sessions cheaper than establishing a new link for every short request?
Why does getting the estimated number of rows wrong lead the optimizer to choose a terrible execution strategy?
What does the COMPACTION_FANOUT_RATIO setting control when merging immutable sorted runs?
How does an idle session left holding an open transaction end up keeping obsolete row versions around and bloating storage?
Why does network throughput collapse when too many senders push data at once, and what makes the sending rate rise and then drop in that repeating sawtooth pattern?
How do a client and a server settle on shared encryption keys without ever transmitting those keys in the open, and confirm they're really talking to who they think?
When a user's session state lives on one particular machine, how do you keep sending that same visitor back to that machine instead of bouncing them around the pool?
Two devices are both sitting behind home routers with no port forwarding. How can they open a direct connection to each other instead of routing everything through a relay?
What does the NEGATIVE_CACHE_TTL_S setting control and why does it matter for repeated failed lookups?
How can returning several IP addresses for a single hostname be used as a cheap way to spread incoming clients across servers?
How do teams decide when to stop shipping risky features and focus on stability instead, based on how much downtime they've already used up?
When a request passes through a bunch of microservices and one hop is slow, how can I see the end-to-end timeline and figure out which service caused the delay?
How do I make my application logs searchable like a database with named fields instead of having to parse free-form text lines with regexes?
Why does adding a label like user id or raw URL to a metric cause the number of stored time series to explode and crash the monitoring backend?
What does the configuration error RED-417 mean and how is it triggered?
How do the request rate and failure counts from everyday service monitoring get used to calculate how much reliability allowance remains?
If I want to store a value so it can never be turned back into the original, but elsewhere I need to scramble a message and recover it later with a key, which approach fits each case?
How do you turn a short, easily-guessed passphrase into a strong random key while making mass cracking expensive and adding per-account randomness?
Why can't an attacker who steals my server's long-term private key go back and decrypt conversations they recorded months ago?
What goes wrong if the same starting value is used twice with one key in a stream or counter cipher, and how do you guarantee it stays distinct every time?
What does the SIG_DIGEST_ALG parameter set when validating a signed message?
Across building secure sessions, why does freshness and single-use of values matter both for the per-message encryption inputs and for the throwaway per-session keys that get wiped afterward?
How can an outside auditor confirm that a shipped binary really came from the published source code without trusting the vendor's build server?
What release approach keeps the previous version of the app fully running so you can switch back to it in seconds if the new one misbehaves?
How do build systems avoid recompiling unchanged modules by looking up saved outputs based on a fingerprint of the inputs?
What lets a team disable a piece of functionality for all users at runtime, from a config store, without pushing a new build?
What does the SEMVER_CONTRACT_BREACH label track and how is it caught?
When a freshly released change starts causing errors in production, what are the fastest ways to mitigate it without doing a full code rollback?
What data structure lets a search engine look up which documents contain a given word without scanning the whole collection?
How do you score a document's relevance by rewarding repeated mentions of a query word while down-weighting words that appear in almost every document?
How is raw text split into words and then reduced so that plurals and past-tense forms collapse to the same root before indexing?
How can a retriever match a passage that answers a question using completely different vocabulary, and quickly find the closest matches among millions of candidates?
What does the RRF_RANK_DAMPING constant control when merging ranked lists?
In a hybrid search setup, how do you blend keyword results with meaning-based semantic results into one final ranking?
How can I measure how long an operation took without getting a negative result when the system time gets corrected backward by a sync daemon?
When thousands of clients all fail at once and retry together, how do I add randomness to their waits so they stop colliding and overwhelming the recovering service?
What data structure always serves the most important task first, and how do you stop the least important items from waiting forever?
Why does an occasional extra second get added to civil time to track the planet's rotation, and what is the smearing technique used to absorb it smoothly?
What does the TOKEN_REFILL_RATE_PER_S setting control in a token bucket?
When a server refuses a request because the client is going too fast, what is the cooperative way the client should wait before trying again, and how does the server-side throttling pair with it?
